query: current evidence on fondaparinux use in perioperative bridging

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Evaluation -- Determine Whether or Not Bridging Anticoagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Bridging anticoagulation consists of the substitution of a long-acting anticoagulant (usually with warfarin) for a shorter-acting anticoagulant (usually LMWH) to limit the time of subtherapeutic anticoagulation levels and minimize thromboembolic risk [31] . Despite the growing evidence about the limited to nonexistent benefits of bridging therapy, it is still being used on a case-by-case basis. Clinical scenarios that may benefit from bridging therapy are those involving patients with high thromboembolic risk. In several guidelines, the following scenarios have been proposed:

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Treatment / Management -- How to bridge
retrieved_docs.content: Our bridging recommendations follow the AT9 guidelines, which agree with the American Society of Regional Anesthesia (ASRA) 2018 guidelines and are similar to the American College of Surgeons 2018 guidelines. During the preoperative period: Discontinue warfarin 5 days before surgery. Three days before surgery, start subcutaneous LMWH or unfractionated heparin (UFH) at therapeutic doses, depending on the patient's renal function. Two days before surgery assess INR, if greater than 1.5 vitamin K can be administered at a dose of 1 to 2 mg. Discontinue LMWH 24 hours before surgery or 4 to 6 hours before surgery if UFH. During the postoperative period:

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Treatment / Management -- How to bridge
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to note that bridging therapy is not indicated in patients on DOACs. The predictable pharmacological effect of DOACs allows a properly timed interruption of anticoagulation therapy before surgery. Various societies have issued recommendations about the timing of DOAC interruption. Our recommendations are based on the American College of Cardiology Expert Consensus (2017), the European Heart and Rhythm Association (2018), and the American Society of Regional Anesthesia (ASRA) (2018). [4] [19] [20] The appropriate timing interruption for patients on DOAC anticoagulation is based on the invasiveness and bleeding risk of the procedure, pharmacokinetic profile of the DOAC, and clinical characteristics of the patient (renal function and liver function). See Table. Timing Interruption of Direct Oral Anticoagulants. As a common recommendation among guidelines, DOACs should be held 3 half-life times before low-risk procedures and 5 half-life times before high-risk procedures. Nevertheless, some procedures are considered less than low bleeding risk, such as a colonoscopy without biopsy, where DOAC therapy may be continued (see Table. Timing Interruption of Direct Oral Anticoagulants). Betrixaban is a new inhibitor of factor Xa. Though many aspects of the clinical use of this drug are still under study, ASRA (2018) recommends betrixaban interruption at least 72 hours before a neuraxial block. ASRA also recommends at least 5 hours between catheter removal and reinitiation of the drug. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Anticoagulation -- Clinical Significance -- Bridging Anticoagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Perioperative and periprocedural anticoagulation management is a constant clinical dilemma for providers. Estimation of the risk of thromboembolism, bleeding, and timing of anticoagulation should be considered for patients undergoing elective procedures. Bridging anticoagulation, most commonly with low molecular weight heparin, should be considered in cases with considerable risk of thromboembolic events. For patients with low risk, bridging anticoagulation is not indicated. However, for patients with significant risk, bridging anticoagulation is indicated. [26] [27] [28] The American College of Cardiology (ACC), American Heart Association (AHA), and HRS (Heart Rhythm Society) recommendations are considered. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Managing patients on anticoagulation and anti-aggregation therapy is a daily challenge for physicians. The interruption of therapy can increase the risk of thrombotic events during and after surgery. However, the non-interruption of these medications can heighten the risk of bleeding during surgery and trigger a sequence of undesirable outcomes ranging from minor to uncontrolled bleeding. The optimal management of these patients is thus achieved through a balance between thromboembolic and bleeding risks. Several case-based considerations affect whether or not to interrupt anticoagulation or anti-aggregation therapy before surgery. These include evaluating an individual's underlying bleeding risk, the risk of bleeding associated with the surgical procedure, the timing of interruption and resumption of anticoagulation therapy, and whether to use bridging therapy. These are all typical questions that can be addressed in this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess the pharmacology that can affect homeostasis in patients undergoing non-cardiac elective surgery. Evaluate the current antithrombotic and anticoagulation interruption indications in patients undergoing non-cardiac elective surgery. Identify the different emergency anticoagulation reversal treatments and their indications. Communicate the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team, which can enhance patient care when dosing and monitoring anticoagulant drugs and improve patient outcomes for patients receiving anticoagulation undergoing non-cardiac elective surgery. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Evaluation -- Determine Whether or Not Bridging Anticoagulation
retrieved_docs.content: In the BRIDGE (Bridging anticoagulation in patients who require temporary interruption of warfarin therapy for elective surgery) study, 1,884 patients with atrial fibrillation requiring interruption of warfarin anticoagulation therapy were allocated to either a dalteparin or placebo group. There was no difference between groups in arterial thromboembolic events at 30 days after surgery (0.3 vs. 0.4). However, the incidence of major bleeding was higher in the dalteparin bridging group (3.2% vs. 1.3%, P <0.01), as was minor bleeding (20.9% vs. 12.0%, P <0.01). This study was criticized for the exclusion of high thromboembolic risk patients. [34] The PERIOP-2 study examined whether there is any benefit of bridging therapy after surgery. This study was a randomized clinical trial of 1,471 enrolled patients, 304 of which had a mechanical valve (99 of these had atrial fibrillation simultaneously). There were no significant differences for major thromboembolic episodes between dalteparin and placebo (0.71% vs. 1.11%, respectively). This study showed no benefit of using dalteparin vs. placebo for episodes of major bleeding during the postoperative period (1.46% vs. 2.46%, respectively).

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: The American Society of Regional Anesthesia and Pain Medicine guidelines recommend LMWH as bridging therapy. Discontinuation of warfarin at least 5 days before a planned procedure is suggested. The international normalized ratio (INR) should be assessed 1 to 2 days before surgery; vitamin K may be considered if the INR exceeds 1.5. For urgent cases, reversal strategies include vitamin K, with immediate options such as prothrombin complex concentrates (PCCs) or fresh frozen plasma. For patients at high risk of thromboembolism, bridging therapy is advised, with subcutaneous low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) preferred. [7] Low-molecular-weight heparin may also be administered for patients who are at risk for recurrent spontaneous abortion. [8] [9] LMWHs are not the sole medications used for these purposes; therefore, an appropriate prescription requires a thorough understanding of the available anticoagulants and their pros and cons.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Treatment / Management -- How to Perform Emergency Reversal of Anticoagulation
retrieved_docs.content: The required time for anticoagulant reversal and INR correction with vitamin K and PCC are 6 to 24 hours and 15 minutes after a 10-minute to 1-hour infusion, respectively. FFP should be administered if ongoing bleeding or PCC is unavailable. The timing of anticoagulation reversal depends on the timing of the complete infusion. Complete reversal usually takes up to 32 hours. PCC must be administered with vitamin K. [39] Reversal of DOACs: The FDA recently approved andexanet alfa for reversing the anticoagulative effects of apixaban and rivaroxaban. [40] The effects of andexanet alfa also seem to be extended to betrixaban and edoxaban; however, more studies are needed. DOAC anticoagulation represents a problem for the clinician. For several years, the first-line option for the reversal of anticoagulation was PCC. However, evidence about prothrombin complex concentrate effectiveness is controversial. With the approval of new specific reversal medications, its future use is uncertain (see Table. Anticoagulation Reversal Agents for Patients on DOACs). What is known is that the cost of the specific new reversal drugs is still high, limiting their use and widespread availability. A complete reversal treatment with andexanet alfa can cost around $50,000, compared to the full treatment with prothrombin complex concentrate, which is about $5,000. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Evaluation -- Determine Whether or Not Bridging Anticoagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Evidence on bridging therapy follows a trend against its use. Sigeal et al (2012) performed a meta-analysis of 34 studies, most of which were observational. Despite the criticism of this meta-analysis for its heterogeneity of studies, interesting conclusions were drawn. Regarding the risk of thromboembolism, there was no statistically significant difference between patients who received bridging therapy and those who did not receive bridging therapy(OR:0.80; 95% CI 0.42-1.54). However, the risk of major bleeding had a threefold increase in patients who received bridging therapy compared with patients who did not (OR: 3.60; 95% CI 1.52-8.50). [32] Large studies of anticoagulation in patients with atrial fibrillation, such as the RE-LY study (warfarin vs. dabigatran), showed that patients on warfarin who received bridging therapy had more thromboembolic events compared with those who did not receive bridging therapy (1.8 vs. 0.3%). Patients who received warfarin plus bridging therapy had a higher risk of major bleeding (warfarin plus bridging therapy 6.8%; warfarin without bridging 1.6%) compared with patients who received dabigatran plus bridging therapy (dabigatran plus bridging therapy 6.5%; dabigatran without bridging therapy 1.8%). [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Treatment / Management -- How to bridge
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is tolerating oral intake, and there are no unexpected surgical issues that would increase bleeding risk, restart warfarin 12 to 24 hours after surgery. If the patient received preoperative bridging therapy (high thromboembolic risk) and underwent a minor surgical procedure, resume LMWH or UFH 24 hours after surgery. If the patient underwent a major surgical procedure, resume LMWH or UFH 48 to 72 hours after surgery. Always assess the bleeding risk and adequacy of homeostasis before the resumption of LMWH or UFH [4] [12] [18] [35] How to Manage Patients on DOAC Anticoagulation Therapy Undergoing Elective S urgery

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Treatment / Management -- How to bridge
retrieved_docs.content: In 2019, a new strategy was published in the PAUSE study, a prospective clinical trial evaluating a standardized approach for perioperative management of DOACs. The interruption scheme used in this study was simple. For high-bleeding-risk procedures, rivaroxaban, apixaban, and dabigatran were suspended 48 hours before surgery in patients with CrCl>50 ml/min. If the renal function was compromised (CrCl< 50 ml/min), these drugs were interrupted for 4 days before surgery. For low-bleeding-risk procedures, rivaroxaban, apixaban, and dabigatran were interrupted 24 hours before surgery in patients with CrCl>50 ml/min. If the renal function was compromised (CrCl <50 ml/min), drugs were suspended 2 days before the procedure. Regardless of renal function, all drugs were reinitiated at 48 hours for high-bleeding-risk surgical procedures and 24 hours for low-bleeding-risk procedures. The 30-day postoperative rate of major bleeding was 1.35% (95% CI, 0%-2.00%), and the rate of arterial thromboembolism was 0.16% (95% CI, 0%-0.48%). However, more studies are needed in patients with high surgical bleeding risk. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Management of perioperative anticoagulation requires an interprofessional approach to determining the duration and discontinuation of temporary anticoagulation interruption. Parenteral anticoagulation during temporary interruption may be required in certain circumstances based on the patient's and procedure's individualized risks and benefits. This activity explains the recommendations for perioperative management of anticoagulation for patients with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation undergoing non-cardiac surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Evaluation -- Determine the Procedural Bleeding Risk
retrieved_docs.content: Implantation of prosthetic cardiac devices is also considered a bleeding risk procedure. Two studies, the BRUISE CONTROL 1 and 2, have addressed this scenario. The first study examined uninterrupted anticoagulation vs. warfarin interruption and a low therapeutic dose of low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) (1 mg per kg/twice daily of enoxaparin) in patients undergoing pacemaker implantation. The patients with uninterrupted warfarin had a lower incidence of pocket hematoma complications compared with the group that received bridging therapy with LMWH (3.5% vs. 16%, P <0.01). [29] The BRUISE CONTROL 2 study evaluated the safety of performing electrophysiologic device procedures without interrupting DOAC anticoagulation therapy. The study compared uninterrupted DOACs vs. interrupted DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran interrupted 2 days before surgery) in patients with high thromboembolic risk. The strategy of continuing DOACs to reduce the complications of pocket hematoma was not superior to the interruption of DOACs 48 hours before the procedure. [30] Additionally, there was no statistically significant difference in other outcomes such as stroke, any hematoma, and any adverse event. This trial was terminated early due to futility.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Anticoagulation Management -- Pathophysiology -- Heparins
retrieved_docs.content: The binding of the heparin molecule to the antithrombin receptor enhances its potency to inactivate factors II and Xa. This drug has been widely used for years, with multiple indications and dosages. One of this pharmacological group's most concerning adverse effects is the possibility of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Fortunately, this complication is infrequent, though it depends on the dose, route of administration, and type of heparin. In patients with renal failure and a CrCl < 30 ml/min, low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) should be avoided or adjusted to renal function. The use of unfractionated heparin (UFH) is a suitable option in this case. Heparin can be administered as a therapeutic dose for patients with high thromboembolic risk (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily or dalteparin 100 units/kg twice daily) or as the prophylactic dose for patients undergoing bridge therapy (enoxaparin 40 mg once daily or dalteparin 5,000 units once daily). [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Cardiac Management -- Issues of Concern -- Antiplatelet Agents
retrieved_docs.content: For patients who need interruption of antiplatelet therapy, it should be 5 days before the surgery for clopidogrel or ticagrelor and 7 days for aspirin and prasugrel. Avoid the use of low-molecular-weight heparin for bridging in these patients. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 29
retrieved_docs.content: general fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis with contrast; percutaneous thrombolysis of the axillary vein; and percutaneous thrombolysis of central venous catheters. In contrast, complex endovascular intracranial interventions described as “prolonged administration of pharmacologic agent(s) other than for thrombolysis, arterial, including catheter placement, diagnostic angiography, and imaging guidance” explicitly exclude fibrinolytic agents, underscoring that not all intracranial endovascular techniques rely on fibrinolysis. Low‑molecular‑weight heparins and related heparin‑group agents—including antithrombin III, dalteparin, nadroparin, tinzaparin and enoxaparin—potentiate antithrombin III activity to inhibit thrombin and factor Xa, providing prophylaxis and therapy for venous thromboembolism and complementing fibrinolytic therapy by preventing new clot formation. Fibrinolytic and heparin‑based drugs are listed among agents capable of causing adverse drug reactions, placing them within

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: endovascular intracranial administration, where non‑thrombolytic pharmacologic agents are explicitly excluded, underscoring the need for agent‑specific selection. Within the broader antithrombotic drug family, direct Factor Xa inhibitors (e.g., rivaroxaban), direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban, dabigatran etexilate) and the heparin group—including unfractionated heparin, low‑molecular‑weight heparins (enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin, tinzaparin) and the cofactor antithrombin III—share the therapeutic goal of preventing clot formation. Ophthalmic agents placed in the ATC subclass “Other ophthalmologicals” include Merkaptamin, inosin, ethylmorfin, potassium iodide (mechanism unknown) and riboflavin, illustrating cross‑disciplinary classification. Safety considerations emphasize accidental poisoning by fibrinolytics and drug‑induced levin dissolution poisoning, highlighting the importance of careful handling and dosing. Together, the interrelated concepts of contrast‑enhanced

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 573
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes unfractionated heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin and tinzaparin, as well as the plasma protein antithrombin III. All of these agents share a common anticoagulant mechanism in which they potentiate antithrombin III to inhibit key clotting factors, thereby preventing thrombus formation. Because of their similar pharmacologic actions, heparin, enoxaparin, dalteparin, tinzaparin and antithrombin III are collectively classified within the Heparin group antithrombotic drugs, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate anticoagulant therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors and Heparin group antithrombotic drugs are both categories of antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors function by inhibiting factor Xa and comprise agents such as rivaroxaban. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs includes low‑molecular‑weight heparins—enoxaparin, dalteparin, and tinzaparin—which achieve anticoagulation primarily through potentiation of antithrombin III. While both classes are employed to prevent and treat thrombotic conditions, they differ in their specific mechanisms of action and the individual drugs that belong to each group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors, Direct thrombin inhibitors, Heparin‑group antithrombotic drugs, and Other antithrombotic agents in ATC are all subclasses of antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors exert their effect by inhibiting factor Xa, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade, and include rivaroxaban as a representative member of this class. Direct thrombin inhibitors act on thrombin to prevent clot formation and comprise drugs such as argatroban and dabigatran etexilát. Heparin‑group antithrombotic drugs enhance the activity of antithrombin III and encompass agents like enoxaparin, dalteparin, tinzaparin, and antithrombin III itself. Other antithrombotic agents in ATC contain drugs such as fondaparinux and dermatansulfát, which are classified separately from the heparin group. Together, these categories provide a spectrum of mechanisms for preventing and treating thrombosis, and recognizing their relationships helps clinicians choose appropriate therapy based on the specific pharmacologic target and drug characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1277
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs constitute a class of anticoagulant agents that exert their effect primarily through interaction with antithrombin III, a natural inhibitor of several clotting factors. This drug class includes low‑molecular‑weight heparins such as dalteparin and tinzaparin, both of which are members of the heparin group and share the mechanism of potentiating antithrombin III activity to prevent thrombus formation. Understanding that dalteparin and tinzaparin belong to the heparin group antithrombotic drugs helps clinicians select appropriate anticoagulant therapy for patients at risk of or experiencing thromboembolic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: indicating dextrany as a more specific anticoagulant entity. Recognizing these hierarchical classifications, dual formulations, and contraindications supports clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies for respiratory disorders, imaging procedures, wound management, and infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are a class of antithrombotic agents that act by inhibiting factor Xa, and this class includes drugs such as rivaroxaban. Heparin group antithrombotic drugs also belong to the antithrombotic agents category; they exert their effect through antithrombin III and comprise specific members such as dalteparin, nadroparin, and tinzaparin. Other antithrombotic agents in the ATC classification are likewise antithrombotic agents and encompass compounds such as fondaparinux and dermatansulfát. Together, these classes and individual agents represent the major pharmacologic options for inhibiting coagulation through different mechanisms—direct factor Xa inhibition, potentiation of antithrombin III, and synthetic pentasaccharide activity—providing clinicians with a range of therapeutic choices for preventing and treating thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 378
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs constitute a class of anticoagulant medications that achieve their therapeutic effect primarily through interaction with antithrombin III, a plasma protein that inhibits clotting factors. Within this drug class, dalteparin and nadroparin are specific low‑molecular‑weight heparin agents that share the same mechanism of enhancing antithrombin III activity to prevent thrombus formation. Recognizing dalteparin and nadroparin as members of the heparin group clarifies their pharmacologic relationship and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate antithrombotic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1972
retrieved_docs.content: Cisaprid is contraindicated with both dextran 40 and dextran 75, meaning that these dextran formulations should not be co‑administered with cisaprid. Dextran 40 and dextran 75 each have antithrombotic activity and may prevent التخثر الوريدي (venous thrombosis). Because of this interaction, clinicians should avoid using cisaprid in patients who are receiving dextran 40 or dextran 75 for thrombosis prophylaxis, ensuring safe medication management and effective prevention of venous thrombotic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs are a class of anticoagulant agents that function primarily by potentiating the activity of antithrombin III, a natural plasma protein that inhibits several clotting factors. Within this group, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin and tinzaparin are recognized as individual members and are widely used for prophylaxis and treatment of thromboembolic disorders. Although antithrombin III is not itself a drug, it is a critical component of the heparin mechanism of action, and its interaction with these heparin agents underlies their therapeutic effect. Understanding that enoxaparin, dalteparin and tinzaparin belong to the heparin antithrombotic class, and that antithrombin III serves as the essential cofactor, is important for appropriate selection and management of anticoagulant therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1141
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs constitute a class of anticoagulant agents that exert their therapeutic effect by potentiating antithrombin III activity, thereby inhibiting clotting factors in the coagulation cascade. This drug class includes several low‑molecular‑weight heparins, specifically dalteparin, nadroparin, and tinzaparin, each of which shares the common mechanism of enhancing antithrombin III–mediated inhibition of thrombin and factor Xa. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same pharmacologic group helps clinicians select appropriate anticoagulant therapy and understand their shared pharmacodynamics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: role in thrombolysis, identifies the imaging procedures that require concurrent use of contrast agents for safe and effective clot removal, and clarifies procedural situations where fibrinolytic use is contraindicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are a subclass of antithrombotic agents that act through an inhibitory faktoru Xa mechanism, thereby preventing the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin. Rivaroxaban is a specific drug that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs also fall under the broader category of antithrombotic agents; they exert their anticoagulant effect primarily by enhancing the activity of antithrombin III and include low‑molecular‑weight heparins such as enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin, and tinzaparin. Other antithrombotic agents listed in the ATC classification comprise fondaparinux, a synthetic pentasaccharide that selectively inhibits factor Xa, and dermatansulfát, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan with anticoagulant properties. Together, Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors, Heparin group antithrombotic drugs, and these other ATC antithrombotic agents represent distinct but related therapeutic options for preventing thrombus formation, each characterized by unique molecular targets and representative drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 543
retrieved_docs.content: The heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class of anticoagulant agents that includes heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as antithrombin III. All of these substances are members of the heparin group antithrombotic drugs and share the common mechanism of potentiating antithrombin activity to inhibit clot formation, making them central to the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as antithrombin III. All of these agents share the common mechanism of potentiating antithrombin activity to inhibit clot formation and are used to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders. In clinical practice, heparin may be employed in the management of cerebral infarction (احتشاء دماغي), underscoring the therapeutic relevance of this drug class for acute stroke and other conditions where rapid anticoagulation is required.
